title: Grid Algorithms & a Match-3 Puzzle
html:
The grid is the game

A Match-3 (think Bejeweled or Candy Crush) is, at heart, a 2D array of integers plus a handful of pure functions that operate on it. The whole game is: swap two cells, find runs of three or more, clear them, let everything above fall, refill from the top, repeat until no more matches. The fun comes from cascades — when a refill creates a new match automatically. Get the grid algorithms right and the rest is animation polish.

We model the board as grid[y][x] where each value is a gem type (0..N-1) or -1 for empty. Keep the data model and the on-screen tween positions separate: the logic snaps instantly, the visuals catch up over time.

Law of grid games.
Mutate the data model immediately and completely, then animate toward the new truth. Never let your tweens be the source of truth — that path leads to desync bugs you can never reproduce.

Building the board

board.js
// 8x8 board, 6 gem types export const W = 8, H = 8, TYPES = 6; const rnd = () => Math.floor(Math.random() * TYPES); export function makeBoard() { const g = []; for (let y = 0; y < H; y++) { g[y] = []; for (let x = 0; x < W; x++) { // avoid spawning a match on initial fill let t; do { t = rnd(); } while ((x >= 2 && g[y][x-1]===t && g[y][x-2]===t) || (y >= 2 && g[y-1][x]===t && g[y-2][x]===t)); g[y][x] = t; } } return g; }

The do/while guard rejects a candidate gem if it would complete a horizontal or vertical run, so the starting board is guaranteed match-free — players hate a board that auto-explodes before they touch it.

Finding matches

Scan each row and each column for runs of 3+ identical values. Collect the coordinates into a Set (keyed by y*W+x) so a gem that is part of both a horizontal and vertical match is only counted once.

matching.js
import { W, H } from './board.js'; export function findMatches(g) { const hits = new Set(); const scan = (read) => { const a = read.outer, b = read.inner; for (let i = 0; i < a; i++) { let run = 1; for (let j = 1; j <= b; j++) { const cur = j < b ? read.at(i, j) : -2; const prev = read.at(i, j - 1); if (cur === prev && cur !== -1) { run++; } else { if (run >= 3) for (let k = j - run; k < j; k++) read.mark(hits, i, k); run = 1; } } } }; // rows scan({ outer: H, inner: W, at:(y,x)=>g[y][x], mark:(s,y,x)=>s.add(y*W+x) }); // columns (swap roles) scan({ outer: W, inner: H, at:(x,y)=>g[y][x], mark:(s,x,y)=>s.add(y*W+x) }); return hits; }

One generic scan serves both axes by passing in accessor functions. That is a tidy way to avoid copy-pasting the run-length loop. The sentinel -2 at the end forces the loop to flush any in-progress run when it reaches the edge.

Gravity & refill

After clearing matched cells (set them to -1), pull surviving gems down each column, then top up the gaps with fresh random gems.

gravity.js
import { W, H, TYPES } from './board.js'; export function clearAndCollapse(g, hits) { for (const key of hits) g[Math.floor(key / W)][key % W] = -1; for (let x = 0; x < W; x++) { let write = H - 1; // bottom-most empty slot for (let y = H - 1; y >= 0; y--) { if (g[y][x] !== -1) { g[write][x] = g[y][x]; if (write !== y) g[y][x] = -1; write--; } } for (let y = write; y >= 0; y--) g[y][x] = Math.floor(Math.random()*TYPES); } }

The write pointer is the classic two-pointer compaction trick: it walks up from the bottom, packing non-empty gems, and whatever rows it never reached become the refill zone.

Cascades & the resolve loop

A single swap can trigger a chain. Loop: find matches; if none, stop. Otherwise clear, collapse, score (bigger chains score more), and go again. Multiply the score by the chain depth to reward cascades.

resolve.js
import { findMatches } from './matching.js'; import { clearAndCollapse } from './gravity.js'; export function resolve(g) { let chain = 0, score = 0; while (true) { const hits = findMatches(g); if (hits.size === 0) break; chain++; score += hits.size * 10 * chain; // cascade multiplier clearAndCollapse(g, hits); } return { score, chain }; } // A swap is only legal if it produces at least one match. export function trySwap(g, x1, y1, x2, y2) { [g[y1][x1], g[y2][x2]] = [g[y2][x2], g[y1][x1]]; if (findMatches(g).size === 0) { [g[y1][x1], g[y2][x2]] = [g[y2][x2], g[y1][x1]]; // revert return null; } return resolve(g); }
Animate the snapshot diff.
Before each clearAndCollapse, copy the grid; after, diff old vs new to know which gems fell how far. Drive a tween (ease-out) from old pixel-Y to new pixel-Y over ~150ms per step. The player sees a smooth cascade even though the data resolved in one synchronous loop.

Input: select then swap

On pointer-down, convert pixel to cell (Math.floor(px/CELL)). First tap selects; second adjacent tap attempts a swap. Reject non-adjacent taps (Manhattan distance must equal 1). If trySwap returns null, play a tiny shake on the two gems to signal an illegal move.

🎮 Build This: Match-3 Puzzle — Gem Cascade

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: the grid/puzzle game. It shares the canvas, loop, input manager and tween helpers from earlier lessons but introduces a pure data-model board.

  1. Render an 8x8 grid of colored rounded rects, one color per gem type, sized to CELL=56px.
  2. Implement click-to-select and adjacent-swap; highlight the selected gem with a border.
  3. Wire trySwap; on success run resolve and update a score readout.
  4. Animate falling gems by diffing the grid before/after each collapse step and tweening pixel-Y with an ease-out.
  5. Add a cascade combo banner ("x3 CHAIN!") that pops when chain > 1.
  6. Bonus: detect a no-moves-left state by scanning every possible swap; if none exist, reshuffle the board.

title: Verlet Physics & a Physics Puzzle
html:
Why Verlet?

For an Angry-Birds-style game you want bouncy, tumbling, stackable bodies without importing a physics engine. Verlet integration is the sweet spot: instead of storing velocity explicitly, each point remembers its previous position. Velocity is implied by pos - prevPos. This makes constraints trivial to enforce — you simply move points and the velocity updates itself — which is why Verlet is the backbone of cloth, rope, ragdolls, and soft-body puzzles.

point.js
// A Verlet point mass: position + implied velocity export function Point(x, y, pinned = false) { return { x, y, px: x, py: y, pinned }; } export function integrate(p, gravity, dt, drag = 0.999) { if (p.pinned) return; const vx = (p.x - p.px) * drag; const vy = (p.y - p.py) * drag; p.px = p.x; p.py = p.y; p.x += vx; p.y += vy + gravity * dt * dt; // a*t^2 because we skip the velocity term }

That single line p.x += p.x - p.px (expanded above) is the whole integrator. Note we multiply gravity by dt*dt — Verlet is a position-based scheme, so accelerations enter quadratically in time. Run with a fixed dt (the fixed-timestep loop from Lesson 1) so behaviour is deterministic.

Distance constraints build everything

A constraint is a rule that two points stay a fixed distance apart. Enforce it by computing the current distance, finding the error, and moving both points half the error toward/away from each other. A rigid box is just four corner points plus six constraints (four edges + two diagonals).

constraint.js
export function Stick(a, b, stiffness = 1) { const dx = b.x - a.x, dy = b.y - a.y; return { a, b, len: Math.hypot(dx, dy), stiffness }; } export function solveStick(s) { const dx = s.b.x - s.a.x, dy = s.b.y - s.a.y; const dist = Math.hypot(dx, dy) || 1e-6; const diff = (s.len - dist) / dist * 0.5 * s.stiffness; const ox = dx * diff, oy = dy * diff; if (!s.a.pinned) { s.a.x -= ox; s.a.y -= oy; } if (!s.b.pinned) { s.b.x += ox; s.b.y += oy; } }
Iterate to converge.
One pass of constraint solving leaves residual error. Run the solver loop several times per frame (5-15 iterations). More iterations = stiffer, less stretchy bodies. This relaxation is what makes Verlet stable where naive springs explode.

The world step

Every frame: integrate all points, then solve all constraints N times, solving wall/ground collisions in the same inner loop so nothing tunnels through the floor.

world.js
import { integrate } from './point.js'; import { solveStick } from './constraint.js'; export function step(world, dt) { const { points, sticks, W, H, gravity = 1400, iters = 12 } = world; for (const p of points) integrate(p, gravity, dt); for (let k = 0; k < iters; k++) { for (const s of sticks) solveStick(s); for (const p of points) { // keep inside bounds if (p.pinned) continue; if (p.y > H) { p.y = H; p.py = p.y + (p.y - p.py) * 0.4; } // bounce w/ friction if (p.x < 0) { p.x = 0; p.px = p.x; } if (p.x > W) { p.x = W; p.px = p.x; } } } }

Reassigning py after clamping is how you set velocity in Verlet: changing the previous position retroactively changes the implied velocity. The 0.4 factor is restitution — lower for dead thuds, higher for trampolines.

Impulse collision between bodies

For body-vs-body you can approximate boxes as circles (one circle per point with a radius) and resolve overlaps by pushing the two points apart along their separation axis — exactly like a constraint, but only applied when they overlap. This gives you good-enough stacking and knock-over physics for a puzzle.

collide.js
export function collideCircles(a, b, ra, rb) { const dx = b.x - a.x, dy = b.y - a.y; const d = Math.hypot(dx, dy) || 1e-6; const overlap = (ra + rb) - d; if (overlap <= 0) return; const nx = dx / d, ny = dy / d, push = overlap / 2; if (!a.pinned) { a.x -= nx*push; a.y -= ny*push; } if (!b.pinned) { b.x += nx*push; b.y += ny*push; } }

The slingshot launch

The projectile is a single point. On pointer-down inside the sling, on drag set a launch vector, on release inject velocity by setting prevPos behind the launch direction: bird.px = bird.x - vx; bird.py = bird.y - vy;. Because velocity is positional, that one assignment is the launch.

Win/lose detection.
Tag certain bodies as targets. Each frame, sum the squared velocity of all points; when total kinetic energy drops below a threshold the world is "at rest" — that is the moment to check whether all targets were destroyed and end the turn.
🎮 Build This: Physics Puzzle — Fortress Fall

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: the physics puzzle. It reuses the fixed-timestep loop and input manager but adds a hand-built Verlet engine.

  1. Build the engine: Point, Stick, and the step world loop with 12 constraint iterations.
  2. Construct stackable crates as 4-point boxes (4 edges + 2 diagonals) and render them as quads.
  3. Add a slingshot: drag to aim, release to launch a projectile point with positional velocity.
  4. Resolve projectile-vs-crate with collideCircles so hits knock the tower over.
  5. Place target blocks; when the world reaches rest, check if all targets fell and show win/turn-over.
  6. Bonus: add a rope (chain of points + sticks) holding a boulder that drops when its anchor stick is hit.

title: Stats, Inventory, Dialogue & an RPG
html:
RPGs are data, not code

The defining trait of an RPG is that the content lives in data, and a small set of generic systems interpret it. A new enemy, item, or quest should be a JSON entry — never a new code path. In this lesson we extend the top-down adventure from Lesson 11 into a full RPG by layering four data-driven systems on top of its movement and tile world: stats, inventory, branching dialogue, and turn-based combat.

Data-driven stats

Model an actor as base stats plus derived stats computed from them and from equipped gear. Deriving on demand means a single formula change updates every character at once.

stats.js
export function makeActor(base) { return { name: base.name, level: base.level, str: base.str, vit: base.vit, agi: base.agi, int: base.int, xp: 0, hp: 0, mp: 0, equip: {}, _maxHp: 0 }; } export function derive(a) { const e = Object.values(a.equip); const sum = (k) => e.reduce((n, it) => n + (it.mods?.[k] || 0), 0); a._maxHp = a.vit * 8 + a.level * 5 + sum('vit') * 8; a.atk = a.str * 2 + sum('atk'); a.def = a.vit + sum('def'); a.speed = a.agi + sum('agi'); if (a.hp === 0 || a.hp > a._maxHp) a.hp = a._maxHp; return a; } export function gainXp(a, amount) { a.xp += amount; const need = a.level * 100; while (a.xp >= need) { a.xp -= need; a.level++; a.str++; a.vit++; } derive(a); }
Separate base from derived.
Store only what designers tune (base stats, levels, equipment ids). Recompute everything else with derive() whenever gear or level changes. Never write a computed value back into a base field — that is how stat bugs and exploits are born.

Inventory & equipment

Items are templates referenced by id; the inventory is a list of stacks. Equipping moves an item reference into actor.equip[slot] and re-derives stats.

inventory.js
export const ITEMS = { rusty_sword: { name:'Rusty Sword', slot:'weapon', mods:{ atk: 4 } }, potion: { name:'Potion', use:(a)=>{ a.hp = Math.min(a._maxHp, a.hp+30); } }, iron_mail: { name:'Iron Mail', slot:'armor', mods:{ def: 6, vit: 2 } }, }; export function addItem(inv, id, n = 1) { const s = inv.find(s => s.id === id); if (s) s.qty += n; else inv.push({ id, qty: n }); } export function equip(actor, inv, id, derive) { const it = ITEMS[id]; if (!it?.slot) return false; actor.equip[it.slot] = it; derive(actor); return true; }

Branching dialogue trees

Dialogue is a graph of nodes: each node has text and a list of choices, each choice pointing to the next node id. Special actions (give item, start fight, set a flag) ride along on the choice. A tiny interpreter walks the graph.

dialogue.js
export const TREE = { start: { text:'Old man: The cave is cursed. Will you help?', choices:[ { label:'Yes', to:'reward' }, { label:'No', to:'bye' } ] }, reward:{ text:'Take this blade, hero.', action:{ give:'rusty_sword' }, choices:[ { label:'Thanks', to:null } ] }, bye: { text:'A coward, then.', choices:[ { label:'...', to:null } ] }, }; export function makeDialog(tree, flags = {}) { let id = 'start'; return { node: () => tree[id], choose(i, ctx) { const c = tree[id].choices[i]; const next = tree[c.to]; if (next?.action?.give) ctx.give(next.action.give); id = c.to; return id !== null; // false = dialogue over } }; }

Gate choices behind flags or stats by adding a cond predicate to a choice and filtering the list before rendering — that is how you build skill checks ("[STR 10] Force the door").

Turn-based combat

Combat is a queue ordered by speed. Each turn the active actor picks an action (attack/skill/item), you apply it, check for death, and advance. Damage uses the derived stats with a little variance.

combat.js
export function order(actors) { return [...actors].sort((a, b) => b.speed - a.speed); } export function attack(att, def) { const raw = att.atk * 2 - def.def; const dmg = Math.max(1, Math.round(raw * (0.85 + Math.random() * 0.3))); def.hp = Math.max(0, def.hp - dmg); return { dmg, dead: def.hp === 0 }; } export function runTurn(state) { // state.queue is the turn order const actor = state.queue[state.turn % state.queue.length]; if (actor.hp <= 0) { state.turn++; return null; } const target = actor.team === 'hero' ? state.enemy : state.hero; const r = attack(actor, target); state.turn++; return { actor, target, ...r }; }
One log, many systems.
Have every system push human-readable strings into a shared log[] array ("Hero hits Slime for 7!", "Found Potion x2"). Render the last few lines in a scrolling box. It is the cheapest way to make an RPG feel alive and to debug all four systems at once.
🎮 Build This: RPG — Cursed Cave

This EXTENDS the top-down adventure from Lesson 11 into a full RPG, reusing its movement, tilemap, and NPC triggers while adding stats, inventory, dialogue, and combat.

  1. Attach a derive()-driven stat sheet to the player; show HP/XP/level on an overlay.
  2. Scatter items as world pickups; add an inventory screen toggled with I and let the player equip gear.
  3. Make an NPC open the branching dialogue tree on interact; deliver a sword reward via a choice action.
  4. Trigger turn-based combat on touching an enemy; render the queue, an action menu, and the shared log.
  5. On victory grant XP through gainXp and watch the level-up message fire automatically.
  6. Bonus: add a STR-gated dialogue choice that only appears when player.str >= 10.

title: Pathfinding, AI & a Tower-Defense Strategy Game
html:
Three systems, one game

A tower-defense game is the intersection of three classic systems: A* pathfinding so creeps navigate a grid maze, a wave manager that spawns enemies over time with rising difficulty, and tower targeting AI that picks who to shoot. Build each independently and the strategy emerges from their interaction.

A* on a grid

A* finds the cheapest path from start to goal by always expanding the node with the lowest f = g + h, where g is the cost so far and h is a heuristic estimate to the goal. On a 4-directional grid the Manhattan distance is the perfect admissible heuristic.

astar.js
// grid: 2D array, 0=open 1=wall. Returns array of {x,y} or null. export function astar(grid, start, goal) { const W = grid[0].length, H = grid.length; const key = (x, y) => y * W + x; const h = (x, y) => Math.abs(x - goal.x) + Math.abs(y - goal.y); const open = [{ ...start, g: 0, f: h(start.x, start.y) }]; const came = new Map(), gScore = new Map([[key(start.x,start.y), 0]]); while (open.length) { open.sort((a, b) => a.f - b.f); // (a real heap is faster) const cur = open.shift(); if (cur.x === goal.x && cur.y === goal.y) return rebuild(came, cur, key); for (const [dx, dy] of [[1,0],[-1,0],[0,1],[0,-1]]) { const nx = cur.x + dx, ny = cur.y + dy; if (nx<0||ny<0||nx>=W||ny>=H||grid[ny][nx]===1) continue; const ng = cur.g + 1; if (ng < (gScore.get(key(nx,ny)) ?? Infinity)) { gScore.set(key(nx,ny), ng); came.set(key(nx,ny), cur); open.push({ x:nx, y:ny, g:ng, f: ng + h(nx,ny) }); } } } return null; } function rebuild(came, cur, key) { const path = [cur]; while (came.has(key(cur.x, cur.y))) { cur = came.get(key(cur.x,cur.y)); path.push(cur); } return path.reverse(); }
Path once, not per-creep-per-frame.
Compute the path when the maze changes (a tower is placed), cache it, and have every creep follow the cached waypoints. Re-running A* for hundreds of creeps every frame will melt the CPU. Also: validate that a new tower never fully blocks the path before allowing the build.

Creeps follow waypoints

A creep stores its path and a target waypoint index. Each frame it steers toward the current waypoint at its speed; on arrival it advances the index. Reaching the end means a life is lost.

creep.js
export function stepCreep(c, dt, cell) { const wp = c.path[c.wp]; if (!wp) { c.reachedGoal = true; return; } const tx = wp.x * cell + cell/2, ty = wp.y * cell + cell/2; const dx = tx - c.x, dy = ty - c.y, d = Math.hypot(dx, dy); const move = c.speed * dt; if (d <= move) { c.x = tx; c.y = ty; c.wp++; } else { c.x += dx/d * move; c.y += dy/d * move; } }

Wave management

A wave is a spawn schedule: how many of each creep type, the interval between spawns, and a delay before the next wave. A small state machine drives it.

waves.js
export function makeDirector(waves) { return { waves, wi: -1, toSpawn: 0, timer: 0, interval: 0, done: false }; } export function updateDirector(d, dt, spawn, fieldClear) { if (d.done) return; if (d.toSpawn === 0 && fieldClear()) { // start next wave d.wi++; if (d.wi >= d.waves.length) { d.done = true; return; } const w = d.waves[d.wi]; d.toSpawn = w.count; d.interval = w.interval; d.hp = w.hp; d.speed = w.speed; d.timer = 0; } if (d.toSpawn > 0) { d.timer -= dt; if (d.timer <= 0) { spawn(d.hp, d.speed); d.toSpawn--; d.timer = d.interval; } } }

Scaling hp and count per wave is your difficulty curve. A common formula: hp = base * 1.25^wave for an exponential ramp that forces players to upgrade towers.

Tower targeting AI

Each tower scans creeps in range and picks one by a policy. The classic policies are first (furthest along the path — kill before it leaks), closest, strongest, and weakest. Then it fires on a cooldown.

tower.js
export function acquire(tower, creeps, policy = 'first') { let best = null, bestScore = -Infinity; for (const c of creeps) { const d = Math.hypot(c.x - tower.x, c.y - tower.y); if (d > tower.range) continue; let score; switch (policy) { case 'first': score = c.wp; break; // most progressed case 'closest': score = -d; break; case 'strongest':score = c.hp; break; case 'weakest': score = -c.hp; break; } if (score > bestScore) { bestScore = score; best = c; } } return best; } export function fireTower(tower, creeps, dt, shoot) { tower.cd -= dt; if (tower.cd > 0) return; const t = acquire(tower, creeps, tower.policy); if (t) { shoot(tower, t); tower.cd = 1 / tower.rate; } }
Projectiles vs hitscan.
Hitscan (instant damage on fire) is simple and reads well for fast towers. For slow, heavy towers spawn a homing projectile that tracks the target — it lets players watch shots miss when a creep dies mid-flight, which makes targeting policy feel meaningful.
🎮 Build This: Strategy / Tower Defense — Maze Keeper

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: strategy / tower-defense. It reuses the grid math from earlier lessons and the object-pool pattern from the twin-stick shooter for projectiles.

  1. Draw a tile grid with a start and goal; let the player place wall-towers on cells by clicking.
  2. Run A* from start to goal and reject any placement that would fully block the path.
  3. Spawn creeps via the wave director and steer them along the cached A* waypoints.
  4. Give towers range, fire rate, and a selectable targeting policy; pool the projectiles.
  5. Track money (earned per kill), lives (lost per leak), and let players buy/upgrade towers.
  6. Bonus: add a slow tower that reduces creep speed and a splash tower that damages all creeps near the impact.

title: Frame Data, Timing Windows: a Fighting Game & a Rhythm Game
html:
Two genres, one obsession: timing

Fighting games and rhythm games look unrelated but share a soul — both are decided by frames and millisecond timing windows. This lesson builds both. The fighter is driven by a per-character state machine with precise hitbox frame data; the rhythm game schedules audio on a sample-accurate clock so notes line up with the music.

Fighting game: states and frame data

Every move is three phases measured in frames: startup (winding up, can't yet hit), active (the hitbox is live), and recovery (can't act, vulnerable). At 60fps a frame is ~16.7ms. "Frame data" is just these numbers — a fast jab might be 3/2/6, a heavy punch 12/4/20.

moves.js
// frames: startup / active / recovery; box is relative to fighter export const MOVES = { jab: { startup:3, active:2, recovery:6, dmg:4, box:{x:30,y:-40,w:40,h:20} }, heavy:{ startup:12, active:4, recovery:20, dmg:14, box:{x:34,y:-30,w:52,h:30} }, low: { startup:6, active:3, recovery:14, dmg:7, box:{x:28,y:-10,w:44,h:18} }, }; export function startMove(f, name) { if (f.state !== 'idle' && f.state !== 'walk') return; // no canceling (yet) f.state = 'attack'; f.move = MOVES[name]; f.frame = 0; }
fighter.js
export function tickFighter(f) { // call once per FIXED frame if (f.state !== 'attack') return null; const m = f.move; f.frame++; const start = m.startup, act = start + m.active; if (f.frame > start && f.frame <= act) { return liveHitbox(f, m); // active frames -> return a hitbox } if (f.frame > act + m.recovery) { f.state = 'idle'; f.move = null; } return null; } function liveHitbox(f, m) { const dir = f.facing; // +1 or -1 return { x: f.x + m.box.x * dir, y: f.y + m.box.y, w: m.box.w, h: m.box.h, dmg: m.dmg, owner: f }; }

Resolve a hit with AABB overlap between an active hitbox and the opponent's hurtbox. Apply damage and hitstun (a few frames where the victim can't act), then check who recovers first — that difference is frame advantage, the core of fighting-game strategy.

Combos = link windows.
A combo is just landing a second move while the opponent is still in hitstun from the first. Tune hitstun frames so that a fast move connects after a slow one but a slow move does not — that single number defines your whole combo system.

Inputs: a buffer for specials

Special moves (quarter-circle, etc.) require detecting a sequence of directions within a time window. Record every input with a timestamp into a ring buffer and pattern-match recent entries.

buffer.js
const buf = []; export function pushInput(token) { buf.push({ token, t: performance.now() }); if (buf.length > 16) buf.shift(); } export function matched(seq, windowMs = 300) { let i = seq.length - 1, now = performance.now(); for (let j = buf.length - 1; j >= 0 && i >= 0; j--) { if (now - buf[j].t > windowMs) break; if (buf[j].token === seq[i]) i--; } return i < 0; // e.g. matched(['down','right','punch']) }

Rhythm game: the Web Audio clock

You cannot schedule beats with setTimeout — it drifts by tens of milliseconds, which is fatal for rhythm. The professional pattern is a look-ahead scheduler: a coarse timer wakes up often and schedules any notes due in the next slice using the sample-accurate AudioContext.currentTime.

rhythm.js
const ac = new AudioContext(); const bpm = 120, beat = 60 / bpm; let nextBeat = 0, beatIndex = 0; const LOOK = 0.1; // schedule 100ms ahead function scheduleBeep(time) { const o = ac.createOscillator(), g = ac.createGain(); o.frequency.value = 880; o.connect(g); g.connect(ac.destination); g.gain.setValueAtTime(0.3, time); g.gain.exponentialRampToValueAtTime(0.001, time + 0.08); o.start(time); o.stop(time + 0.09); } export function scheduler(notes, onSpawn) { while (nextBeat < ac.currentTime + LOOK) { if (notes[beatIndex]) { scheduleBeep(nextBeat); onSpawn(nextBeat, beatIndex); } nextBeat += beat; beatIndex++; } } setInterval(() => scheduler(window.NOTES || [], window.spawnNote || (()=>{})), 25);
Audio time is the only clock.
Judge the player's tap accuracy against ac.currentTime versus the note's scheduled time, never against performance.now() or frame counts. The audio hardware clock is the only reference that stays in lockstep with the sound the player actually hears.

Judging the hit

When the player presses on a note, compute the error against the nearest note time and bucket it into windows.

judge.js
export function judge(noteTime) { const err = Math.abs(ac.currentTime - noteTime) * 1000; // ms if (err <= 30) return { grade:'PERFECT', score:300 }; if (err <= 70) return { grade:'GREAT', score:200 }; if (err <= 120) return { grade:'GOOD', score:100 }; return { grade:'MISS', score:0 }; }
🎮 Build This: Fighting + Rhythm — Two Mini-Games

This STARTS A NEW GENRE pair: a fighting game and a rhythm game (two separate games sharing the engine, input manager, and fixed-timestep loop).

  1. Fighter: build the fighter state machine with jab/heavy/low using the frame-data table; run it on the fixed timestep.
  2. Resolve hitbox-vs-hurtbox overlaps, apply damage + hitstun, and show health bars and a round timer.
  3. Add the input ring buffer and a quarter-circle special that fires a projectile.
  4. Rhythm: build the look-ahead scheduler driving notes from a beat array at 120 BPM.
  5. Spawn falling note sprites synced to scheduled times; judge taps against ac.currentTime into PERFECT/GREAT/GOOD/MISS.
  6. Bonus: add a combo counter to the rhythm game and frame-advantage display to the fighter.

title: Procedural Generation & a Roguelike
html:
What makes a roguelike

The genre's pillars are procedural generation (every run is a fresh dungeon), permadeath (death is final, runs are short and tense), turn-based movement on a grid, and field of view (you only see what you can see). We will build all four. The world is a tile grid of walls and floors; everything else is entities sitting on it.

Generate rooms and corridors

The simplest robust dungeon generator: start with a solid wall grid, carve a handful of non-overlapping rectangular rooms, then connect each new room's center to the previous one with an L-shaped corridor.

dungeon.js
export const WALL = 1, FLOOR = 0; export function generate(W, H, tries = 30) { const map = Array.from({length:H}, () => Array(W).fill(WALL)); const rooms = []; const carve = (r) => { for (let y=r.y; y<r.y+r.h; y++) for (let x=r.x; x<r.x+r.w; x++) map[y][x]=FLOOR; }; const hCorr = (x1,x2,y) => { for (let x=Math.min(x1,x2); x<=Math.max(x1,x2); x++) map[y][x]=FLOOR; }; const vCorr = (y1,y2,x) => { for (let y=Math.min(y1,y2); y<=Math.max(y1,y2); y++) map[y][x]=FLOOR; }; for (let i = 0; i < tries; i++) { const w = 4 + (Math.random()*6|0), h = 4 + (Math.random()*6|0); const x = 1 + (Math.random()*(W-w-2)|0), y = 1 + (Math.random()*(H-h-2)|0); const r = { x, y, w, h }; if (rooms.some(o => x<o.x+o.w+1 && x+w+1>o.x && y<o.y+o.h+1 && y+h+1>o.y)) continue; // overlap carve(r); if (rooms.length) { const p = rooms[rooms.length-1]; const cx = x+(w>>1), cy = y+(h>>1), px = p.x+(p.w>>1), py = p.y+(p.h>>1); if (Math.random()<0.5) { hCorr(px,cx,py); vCorr(py,cy,cx); } else { vCorr(py,cy,px); hCorr(px,cx,cy); } } rooms.push(r); } return { map, rooms }; }
Seed your RNG.
Swap Math.random for a seedable generator (e.g. mulberry32). Then a 32-bit seed reproduces an entire dungeon — priceless for debugging, daily-challenge runs, and sharing a brutal seed with friends. The whole world becomes a single number.

Turn-based scheduling

Nothing moves in real time. The world advances exactly one tick whenever the player takes an action; then every monster gets a turn. This is the energy/turn loop in its simplest form.

turns.js
export function playerAct(world, dx, dy) { const moved = tryMove(world, world.player, dx, dy); if (!moved) return false; // invalid -> no turn passes for (const m of world.monsters) monsterTurn(world, m); world.turn++; return true; } function tryMove(world, e, dx, dy) { const nx = e.x + dx, ny = e.y + dy; const blocker = world.monsters.find(m => m.x===nx && m.y===ny && m.hp>0); if (blocker) { bump(e, blocker, world); return true; } // attack = a turn if (world.map[ny][nx] === 0) { e.x = nx; e.y = ny; return true; } return false; }

Field of view by ray casting

Reveal only tiles the player can see. A clean approach: cast a ray to every tile on the edge of a square radius; walk each ray with Bresenham and stop at the first wall. Tiles touched are visible; tiles ever seen become explored (dimmed when out of sight).

fov.js
export function computeFOV(map, px, py, radius, seen, visible) { visible.clear(); for (let a = 0; a < 360; a += 2) { const rad = a * Math.PI / 180, dx = Math.cos(rad), dy = Math.sin(rad); let ox = px + 0.5, oy = py + 0.5; for (let i = 0; i < radius; i++) { ox += dx; oy += dy; const tx = ox|0, ty = oy|0; if (ty<0||tx<0||ty>=map.length||tx>=map[0].length) break; const k = ty * map[0].length + tx; visible.add(k); seen.add(k); if (map[ty][tx] === 1) break; // wall blocks the ray } } }

Render rule: visible tiles full-bright, explored-but-not-visible tiles dark, never-seen tiles black. Monsters draw only when their tile is in visible — that fog of war is the dread engine of the genre.

Permadeath & monster AI

Monsters use a cheap chase: if the player is within FOV range, step toward them (greedy on the larger axis); otherwise wander or idle. When the player's HP hits zero, the run ends — wipe the world, increment a run counter, and regenerate from a new seed. No saves, no second chances.

Permadeath means no undo.
Resist adding a load-on-death feature "just for testing." The tension of a roguelike is entirely emotional and it evaporates the moment death is reversible. Use seeded runs for debugging instead so you can replay a death without removing its sting.
🎮 Build This: Roguelike — One Last Descent

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: the roguelike. It reuses the tile-grid renderer and turn/grid-movement ideas from the top-down lessons but adds procedural generation and FOV.

  1. Generate a dungeon with the rooms-and-corridors algorithm and place the player in the first room.
  2. Implement turn-based movement where each player step gives every monster one turn.
  3. Add ray-cast FOV with visible/explored/unseen rendering states (fog of war).
  4. Give monsters greedy chase AI and bump-to-attack combat with HP.
  5. Scatter pickups and stairs; descending generates a new, harder level.
  6. Bonus: swap in a seeded RNG so a typed seed reproduces the whole dungeon, and show the seed + run number on death.

title: Cellular Automata & a Simulation / Management Game
html:
Simulations think in ticks

A management/simulation game (think SimCity or Factorio in miniature) is a world that updates on a steady tick, independent of render rate, applying local rules to every cell or agent. Out of thousands of tiny local decisions emerges complex global behaviour. This lesson builds a tick-based sim with cellular-automata rules, an economy loop, and a save system.

The fixed tick

Decouple simulation speed from frame rate with an accumulator (the same pattern as the physics loop, but slower — maybe 4 ticks per second). This lets you add pause and fast-forward by simply scaling how many ticks you run per real second.

tick.js
export function makeClock(tps = 4) { return { acc: 0, step: 1 / tps, speed: 1, tick: 0 }; } export function advance(clock, dt, onTick) { if (clock.speed === 0) return; // paused clock.acc += dt * clock.speed; // speed 2 = fast-forward while (clock.acc >= clock.step) { clock.acc -= clock.step; onTick(clock.tick++); } }
Render reads, tick writes.
Only the tick function may mutate the world; render only reads it. Keep that wall absolute and your sim stays deterministic, pausable, and serializable — and a save taken mid-frame is always consistent.

Cellular automata: double buffering

CA rules read a cell's neighbours and produce its next state. The cardinal rule: compute the entire next grid from the current grid, then swap. If you mutate in place, later cells see already-updated neighbours and the whole simulation becomes order-dependent garbage. Here is a forest-growth/fire CA — a great management hazard system.

forest.js
export const EMPTY = 0, TREE = 1, FIRE = 2; export function tickForest(cur, next, W, H, pGrow = 0.01, pLight = 0.0005) { for (let y = 0; y < H; y++) for (let x = 0; x < W; x++) { const c = cur[y*W+x]; let n = c; if (c === FIRE) n = EMPTY; // burned out else if (c === TREE) { let burning = false; for (let dy=-1; dy<=1; dy++) for (let dx=-1; dx<=1; dx++) { const ny=y+dy, nx=x+dx; if (ny>=0&&nx>=0&&ny<H&&nx<W && cur[ny*W+nx]===FIRE) burning = true; } n = (burning || Math.random()<pLight) ? FIRE : TREE; } else if (c === EMPTY && Math.random() < pGrow) n = TREE; // regrow next[y*W+x] = n; } } // usage: tickForest(a,b,...); [a,b] = [b,a]; // swap buffers

Two flat Uint8Arrays and a swap. Flat typed arrays are both fast and trivially serializable, which matters once the grid is large.

Economy & resource loops

Management depth comes from feedback loops: buildings consume inputs and produce outputs each tick; money flows from production minus upkeep. Model resources as a plain object and run producers over it.

economy.js
export const BUILDINGS = { farm: { cost:{ gold:50 }, in:{ water:1 }, out:{ food:3 }, upkeep:1 }, well: { cost:{ gold:30 }, in:{}, out:{ water:2 }, upkeep:1 }, market: { cost:{ gold:80 }, in:{ food:2 }, out:{ gold:6 }, upkeep:0 }, }; export function tickEconomy(res, placed) { for (const b of placed) { const def = BUILDINGS[b.type]; if (Object.entries(def.in).every(([k,v]) => (res[k]||0) >= v)) { for (const [k,v] of Object.entries(def.in)) res[k] -= v; for (const [k,v] of Object.entries(def.out)) res[k] = (res[k]||0) + v; } res.gold = (res.gold||0) - def.upkeep; // upkeep always due } return res; }

A building only produces if its inputs are available — that scarcity is what forces the player to balance a supply chain (wells feed farms, farms feed markets, markets make gold to buy more wells).

The save system

Because the entire world is plain data (resources object, building list, flat grid arrays, clock tick), saving is just JSON.stringify into localStorage. Typed arrays need converting to plain arrays.

save.js
const KEY = 'sim.save.v1'; export function save(world) { const data = { res: world.res, placed: world.placed, tick: world.clock.tick, grid: Array.from(world.grid), W: world.W, H: world.H, }; localStorage.setItem(KEY, JSON.stringify(data)); } export function load() { const raw = localStorage.getItem(KEY); if (!raw) return null; const d = JSON.parse(raw); d.grid = Uint8Array.from(d.grid); return d; }
Version your save key.
Put a version in the storage key (sim.save.v1). When your data shape changes, bump to v2 and write a tiny migration (or, early in development, just ignore old saves). This prevents the classic "updated the game, all saves crash" disaster.
🎮 Build This: Simulation / Management — Tiny Township

This STARTS A NEW GENRE: the simulation/management sandbox. It reuses the fixed-step accumulator from Lesson 1 (slowed to a sim tick) and the tile grid renderer.

  1. Build the sim clock with pause / 1x / 2x speed controls driven by the accumulator.
  2. Add a double-buffered cellular-automata forest layer (grow, ignite, burn out) running each tick.
  3. Let the player place farms, wells, and markets on the grid, paying gold from a resource pool.
  4. Run the economy each tick so supply chains produce food, water, and gold with upkeep.
  5. Display resources and warn when gold trends negative (a failing economy).
  6. Add save/load to localStorage with a versioned key; auto-save every N ticks.

title: Advanced Canvas Rendering, Web Audio Mastery & an Intro to WebGL
html:
Beyond fillRect

You have shipped a dozen genres with the 2D Canvas API. This lesson levels up the presentation layer: compositing modes for glows and lighting, offscreen canvases for caching and post-processing, a proper Web Audio graph for layered, mixable sound, and finally your first WebGL shader for effects the 2D context simply cannot do at speed.

Compositing for free effects

The globalCompositeOperation property changes how new drawing blends with existing pixels. 'lighter' additively blends — perfect for fire, lasers, and bloom, where overlapping lights get brighter. 'destination-out' erases, which gives you cheap dynamic lighting: fill the screen black, then punch holes of light.

glow.js
export function drawGlow(ctx, x, y, r, color) { ctx.save(); ctx.globalCompositeOperation = 'lighter'; // additive const g = ctx.createRadialGradient(x, y, 0, x, y, r); g.addColorStop(0, color); g.addColorStop(1, 'rgba(0,0,0,0)'); ctx.fillStyle = g; ctx.beginPath(); ctx.arc(x, y, r, 0, Math.PI*2); ctx.fill(); ctx.restore(); // ALWAYS restore the composite mode }
save/restore around state changes.
Composite mode, transforms, alpha, and clip are sticky global state on the context. Forgetting to restore() after changing them is the number-one source of "why is my whole screen suddenly additive?" bugs. Wrap every effect in save()/restore().

Offscreen canvases: cache and post-process

An OffscreenCanvas (or just a detached <canvas>) is a drawing surface you can render to once and blit many times. Two huge uses: cache expensive static art (a parallax background, a complex sprite) so you draw it once and copy it cheaply every frame; and post-processing, where you render the whole game to an offscreen buffer, then draw that buffer to screen with effects applied.

layers.js
const scene = new OffscreenCanvas(960, 540); const sctx = scene.getContext('2d'); export function frame(screenCtx, drawWorld) { // 1) render the game once into the offscreen buffer sctx.clearRect(0, 0, scene.width, scene.height); drawWorld(sctx); // 2) composite to screen (here: a simple darken-vignette pass) screenCtx.drawImage(scene, 0, 0); const v = screenCtx.createRadialGradient(480,270,200, 480,270,520); v.addColorStop(0,'rgba(0,0,0,0)'); v.addColorStop(1,'rgba(0,0,0,0.55)'); screenCtx.fillStyle = v; screenCtx.fillRect(0,0,960,540); }

The Web Audio graph

Up to now a beep was one oscillator. Real game audio is a graph of nodes: sources connect through gain (volume) and effect nodes into a master bus. Build buses for music and SFX so you can mix and duck them independently.

audiograph.js
const ac = new AudioContext(); export const master = ac.createGain(); master.connect(ac.destination); export const sfxBus = ac.createGain(); sfxBus.connect(master); export const musicBus = ac.createGain(); musicBus.connect(master); musicBus.gain.value = 0.5; export function playSfx(buffer) { const src = ac.createBufferSource(); src.buffer = buffer; src.connect(sfxBus); src.start(); } // duck music while an SFX plays export function duck() { const now = ac.currentTime; musicBus.gain.cancelScheduledValues(now); musicBus.gain.setValueAtTime(musicBus.gain.value, now); musicBus.gain.linearRampToValueAtTime(0.15, now + 0.05); musicBus.gain.linearRampToValueAtTime(0.5, now + 0.4); }
Unlock audio on first input.
Browsers start the AudioContext suspended until a user gesture. Call ac.resume() inside your first click/keydown handler, or all your sound silently fails. A one-liner in the input manager solves it forever.

Your first WebGL shader

WebGL runs programs on the GPU. The minimum is two shaders: a vertex shader placing geometry, and a fragment shader computing the color of every pixel in parallel — that parallelism is why GPUs do full-screen effects effortlessly. Here is a full-screen plasma in a fragment shader.

plasma.glsl
// fragment shader (GLSL) precision mediump float; uniform vec2 u_res; uniform float u_time; void main() { vec2 uv = gl_FragCoord.xy / u_res; float v = sin(uv.x * 10.0 + u_time) + sin(uv.y * 10.0 + u_time * 1.3) + sin((uv.x + uv.y) * 8.0 - u_time); vec3 col = 0.5 + 0.5 * cos(vec3(0.0, 2.0, 4.0) + v); gl_FragColor = vec4(col, 1.0); }
gl.js
export function compile(gl, type, src) { const s = gl.createShader(type); gl.shaderSource(s, src); gl.compileShader(s); if (!gl.getShaderParameter(s, gl.COMPILE_STATUS)) throw new Error(gl.getShaderInfoLog(s)); // read the error! return s; } export function program(gl, vsSrc, fsSrc) { const p = gl.createProgram(); gl.attachShader(p, compile(gl, gl.VERTEX_SHADER, vsSrc)); gl.attachShader(p, compile(gl, gl.FRAGMENT_SHADER, fsSrc)); gl.linkProgram(p); if (!gl.getProgramParameter(p, gl.LINK_STATUS)) throw new Error(gl.getProgramInfoLog(p)); return p; } // draw a full-screen quad (two triangles), set u_time each frame, gl.drawArrays

The vertex shader just emits a full-screen quad; the fragment shader does all the work, once per pixel, on the GPU. From here you can pipe your offscreen 2D buffer in as a texture and write shader post-effects (CRT scanlines, chromatic aberration, bloom) over any game you have built.

🎮 Build This: Advanced Rendering / Audio — FX Lab

This is a CONCEPT lesson, not a new genre: an effects lab you can bolt onto any prior game. Pick a finished game (the twin-stick shooter or raycaster works great) and upgrade its presentation.

  1. Add additive 'lighter' glows to bullets/explosions and a destination-out light mask for darkness.
  2. Render the whole game to an OffscreenCanvas, then composite to screen with a vignette post-pass.
  3. Build a Web Audio graph with master/music/SFX buses; duck the music on big hits and unlock audio on first input.
  4. Create a WebGL canvas behind the game and run the plasma fragment shader as an animated background.
  5. Bonus: feed your offscreen 2D buffer into the shader as a texture and apply a CRT scanline + chromatic-aberration post-effect to the entire game.
